0: The impact of feeding growing-finishing pigs with reduced dietary protein levels on performance, carcass traits, meat quality and environmental impacts
A.N.T.R. Monteiro ³,1. , T.M. Bertolb, P.A.V. de Oliveirab, J.-Y. Dourmad A. Coldebella ¹, A.M. Kessler

<list>a Animal Science Department, Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Sul, 91540-000 Porto Alegre, RS, Brazil 
b Embrapa Suínos e Aves, 89700-000 Concórdia, SC, Brazil 
c INRA Agrocampus Ouest, UMR 1348 Pegase, 35590 Saint-Gilles, France</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Environment Fattening pigs Life cycle assessment Modelling Nutrient supplies|The objective of this study was to evaluate the effect of reducing dietary nutrient content for pigs from 25 to 130 kg live weight, on performance, carcass traits, meat quality and environmental impact. Forty gilts and 40 barrows were distributed in a randomized block design with two treatments and 10 replications per treatment, with four animals per experimental unit. The feeding program was in four phases. Two diets were formulated for each feeding phase. One was adjusted using the InraPorc® model to minimize crude protein, amino acid and phosphorus excess (LN), and the other (ST) was formulated with standard Brazilian recommendations. No differences were found on performance. The mean ADG and ADFI were 0.919 and 2.46 kg/day, respectively. Carcass characteristics and meat quality were also not affected by the experimental diets. The average total feed cost was 6.8% lower (P 0.05) for animals fed the LN diets. For nitrogen and phosphorus balance, there was no statistical difference in retention, but the nitrogen and phosphorus intake were 15.8% and 9.42% lower for pigs fed LN diets, respectively, and the excretion levels were 24.1% and 14.6% lower for pigs fed LN diets, respectively. Life cycle assessment showed that LN strategy can reduce the environmental impacts of climate change and terrestrial ecotoxicity by about 4%, acidification and eutrophication by 8% and 10%, respectively, and land occupation by 9%. Data suggest that nutritional adjustment is a valuable alternative to standard formulations, without affecting performance, but lowering costs and reducing environmental burdens.
1: Optimizing nutrient efficiency is essential to increase pig chain sustainability, since it may reduce nutrient excretion and production costs, at the same time meeting government environmental policies (Jean dit Bailleul et al., 2000; Hauschild et al., 2010). Several studies have indicated that nutrient efficiency could be improved by better adjusting the nutrient supply to pig requirements (Hauschild et al., 2012; Pomar et al., 2014; Andretta et al., 2014), via more precise knowledge of the metabolic availability of dietary nutrients and a proper definition of requirements, as well as by the use of highly digestible ingredients and phytase (Miller et al., 2016).
In practice, the approach used to estimate the nutrient require- ments of pigs is often based on empirical methods and average recommendations. It is the case in Brazil where nutritional recom-

mendations are derived from the response of animals, in growth performance studies, to increasing levels of the studied nutrient (Sakomura and Rostagno, 2016). However, in practical situations feed intake and daily weight gain may evolve differently due to differences in climatic conditions, animal health status or genetic potential. Moreover, Hauschild et al. (2010) indicated that the amount of digestible lysine required for the optimal feed conversion ratio of a given population can be lower than the amount required for maximal average daily weight gain.

In this context, modelling appears an interesting approach to enable a more dynamic and adaptive determination of nutrient requirements (van Milgen et al., 2008), compared to standard tabu- lated recommendations. The determination of nutritional requirements for a given herd and a given genotype can be better adjusted by using decision-support tools based on these models, such as InraPorc®

software. This makes it more efficient than empirical models for nutrient requirement estimation. Besides, use of phytase has been consolidated as an efficient tool to improve phosphorus utilization, while reducing phosphorus excretion (Lei et al., 2013). Moreover, environmental restrictions are now being imposed on new pig farms in many countries, compelling the pig industry to balance the amount of nutrients applied to soil as manure against the amount extracted by crops. For instance, in Brazil, through the Ministry of Agriculture, Livestock and Supply, the Brazilian government proposed the ABC Plan (Agriculture Low Carbon) in 2010, in order to plan actions for the adoption of sustainable production technologies, focusing on pig production with low carbon emission (Brasil, 2012).

Reducing the excretion of excess nutrients and restricting the use of non-renewable resource could be essential components in the devel- opment of sustainable pig production (Dourmad and Jondreville, 2007). However, nutritional requirements are still not estimated using mathematical models in the Brazilian industry, because nutrient excess ensures that there are no underfed pigs. Indeed, empirical guidelines express nutrient recommendations with safety margins instead of nutrient requirements, resulting often in a surplus of nutrient supply. In addition to performance, carcass and meat quality are also key factors of concern in the pig industry, since the reduction of some nutrients, such as protein (CP), could increase fat deposition (Pomar et al., 2014; Andretta et al., 2014), due to the fact that low CP diets provide more net energy because of reduced amino acids deamination, reduced urea excretion and lower heat production (Noblet et al., 2001).

This study was thus undertaken to evaluate if a feeding strategy of growing-finishing pigs based on the modelling of nutrient requirements according to a target of performance could achieve at least similar growth performance, carcass traits and meat quality as a strategy based on standard recommendations with rather large safety margins, whilst reducing the environmental impacts evaluated by life cycle assessment.

6: Eighty 68-day-old crossbred pigs (MS-115x(LWxLD)), 40 barrows and 40 gilts, with an average initial weight of 24.5 + 1.79 kg were used. The terminal sire line Embrapa MS-115 is the synthetic line of Pietrain (62.5%), Large White (18.8%) and Duroc (18.8%) breeds, developed at Embrapa Swine and Poultry. The pigs had free access to feed and water throughout the experiment and were cared for in accordance with Brazilian guidelines reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of the Embrapa Swine and Poultry (protocol No. 002/2013). The Brazilian guidelines are based on Federal Law No. 11794 of October 8, 2008.

Pigs were weighed and allotted in a randomized complete block design, blocked by initial body weight within sex. Animals were assigned to one of 20 pens, each one housing four animals (housed separately according to gender), totaling 10 replications per treatment. The pen mx2.40 m) designed with partially slatted floor in a naturally ventilated room, i.e. with large windows controlled by curtains, was considered the experimental unit. Four datalogers (Model Testo 135H; Testo Inc.), two indoors and two outdoors, were used to record the temperature and relative humidity of the environ- ment. Feed was provided with one semi-automatic feeders (Model CAP 1BT, Suin) per pen and water with one low-pressure nipple drinker (Model eco cup, Suin).

The experiment was divided into four phases over the growing- finishing period, with a specific diet for each phase: phase 1 (from 25 to 50 kg BW), phase 2 (from 50 to 80 kg BW), phase 3 (from 80 to 105 kg BW) and phase 4 (from 105 to 130 kg BW). The growing period was considered from 25 to 80 kg BW (from 0 to 63 d) and the finishing period was from 80 to 130 kg BW (from 64 to 112 d).
Table 1 Ingredient composition and nutrient content of the experimental diets (as fed basis).

a Content/kg of feed: Zn, 0.1 g; Cu, 0.01 g; Fe, 0.1 g; Mn, 0.04 g; I, 1.5 mg; Co, 1 mg. b Content/kg of feed: Vit. D3, 225 IU, Vit. E, 22.5 IU, Vit. K3, 2.25 mg, Vit. B1, 2.03 mg, Vit B2, 6 mg, Vit B6, 3 mg, Vit. B12, 30 mcg, Pantothenic Ac., 14 mg, Niacin, 0.03 g, Folic Ac., 0.9 mg, Se, 0.45 mg, Biotin, 0.12 mg, Vit. A, 9000 IU. c Content/kg of feed: 0.5 FTU (Natuphos BASF Chemical Company). d Analyzed composition. e SID=standardized ileal digestible. Calculated from Rostagno et al. (2011). f Digestible P. Calculated from Rostagno et al. (2011).

The pigs had free access to feed and water throughout the experiment and were cared for in accordance with Brazilian guidelines reviewed and approved by the Ethics Committee of the Embrapa Swine and Poultry (protocol No. 002/2013). The Brazilian guidelines are based on Federal Law No. 11794 of October 8, 2008.

Pigs were weighed and allotted in a randomized complete block design, blocked by initial body weight within sex. Animals were assigned to one of 20 pens, each one housing four animals (housed separately according to gender), totaling 10 replications per treatment. The pen mx2.40 m) designed with partially slatted floor in a naturally ventilated room, i.e. with large windows controlled by curtains, was considered the experimental unit. Four datalogers (Model Testo 135H; Testo Inc.), two indoors and two outdoors, were used to record the temperature and relative humidity of the environ- ment. Feed was provided with one semi-automatic feeders (Model CAP 1BT, Suin) per pen and water with one low-pressure nipple drinker (Model eco cup, Suin).

The experiment was divided into four phases over the growing- finishing period, with a specific diet for each phase: phase 1 (from 25 to 50 kg BW), phase 2 (from 50 to 80 kg BW), phase 3 (from 80 to 105 kg BW) and phase 4 (from 105 to 130 kg BW). The growing period was considered from 25 to 80 kg BW (from 0 to 63 d) and the finishing period was from 80 to 130 kg BW (from 64 to 112 d).

13: Two dietary treatments were evaluated (Table 1): a standard (ST) strategy with diets formulated with the nutritional recommendations for barrows with superior performance, as published in Rostagno et al. (2011); and a low nutrient (LN) strategy with diets formulated with the nutritional requirements estimated using the InraPorc® model (version 1.6.5.3; Institut National de la Recherche Agronomique, Saint-Gilles, France). Crude protein (nitrogen6.25), standardized ileal digestible amino acids (SID AA) and digestible phosphorus requirements were used for the feed formulation, with the same metabolizable energy (ME) content between treatments, within each phase. Phytase enzyme was added to LN diets at finisher phases, to keep the line of nutrient reduction. Ingredients were analyzed and their composition was added to the InraPorc® feed database, as well as into the linear least-cost diet formulation software. For maize and soybean meal, standardized digestibility of AA was calculated from total content as in Rostagno et al. (2011).
Diets were mixed twice for each phase, totaling eight batches per diet over the trial. When fed, diets were sampled and one sample was analyzed for each batch of feed.

To determine the nutritional requirements by InraPorc® software, an animal profile was created based on characteristics such as age, feed intake and weight gain (Table 2), following the performance data proposed by Rostagno et al. (2011). Ad libitum feed intake was modeled as a linear function of BW (a+b*BW), being 'a' (dimension- less) equal to 0.832 and 'b' (per kg BW) equal to 0.021. Protein
Table 2 Performance data used to adjust description parameters of animal profile in InraPorc®

a From these data Ad libitum feed intake was modeled as a linear function of BW (a +b*BW) (van Milgen et al., 2008). Protein deposition (PD) was modeled by a Gompertz function described using mean PD (134.5 g/day) and precocity (0.0124 days) (van Milgen et al., 2008).
b Performance data were based on Rostagno et al. (2011).

deposition was modeled by a Gompertz function described using mean PD (134.5 g/day) and precocity (0.0124 days) data. More information on these parameters can be found in van Milgen et al. (2008). The growth curve was parameterized using these parameters and using a reference diet, which was formulated for each one of four phases, following Rostagno et al. (2011) recommendations. From there, a simulation was conducted to obtain the nutrient requirement of the herd, which was considered as proposed by van Milgen et al. (2008) to be 110% of the mean animal requirement. The diets calculated for the LN strategy resulted in a lower soybean meal inclusion, due to the lower amino acid and CP requirements. Thus, more maize was included and resulted in a slightly higher ME content than recommended in the Brazilian table MJ/kg). Therefore, the standard diets were formulated to have the same ME content as the low nutrient diet, and to meet the nutritional requirements proposed by Rostagno et al. (2011).

17: Pigs were weighed at arrival (on d 0), during the fattening period (on d 43, 63 and 91) and at the end of the performance trial (on d 112). Feed intake was recorded over each period for each experimental unit; the leftover feed was weighed and subtracted from the total feed offered during the period. The data collected were used to calculate the average daily gain (ADG), average daily feed intake (ADFI) and feed conver- sion ratio (FCR). Backfat thickness and loin muscle depth (longissimus thoracis) were measured at d 63 and d 112 from images between the 7th and 8th thoracic vertebrae, using ultrasound equipment (Aloka model, SSD 500 V, coupled with a linear probe USF-5011U-3.5, Aloka, USA) and by using the Biosoft Toolbox II software for swine (Biotronics Inc, USA).
18: <header>2.4. Slaughter and carcass and meat evaluation </header>

At the end of the experiment all pigs were weighed and, after 12 h of fasting, were transported to a commercial slaughterhouse, located approximately 15 km from the experimental facilities. Pigs were slaughtered after 4 h of lairage, following the standard procedure of the slaughterhouse. After exsanguination and evisceration the car- casses were driven to a chilling room set for 2-4 °C. Twenty four hours after slaughter, a cut was made between the last thoracic and the first lumbar vertebra of the left half carcass, where the loin eye area and the fat area were evaluated by drawing the outline of the muscle and the fat at the tenth rib on a paper, according to the Brazilian Method of Pig Carcass Grading System (ABCS, 1973). The drawings were scanned and the loin eye area and fat area, as well as the muscle depth and backfat

thickness, were determined using the Rhinoceros® 4.0 software (Robert McNeel and Associates, 2007). From these data the lean percentage (LP) of pig carcasses was calculated, according to the following equation LP=58.408-(0.5886xBackfat)+(0.1739xMuscle depth) (0.0189xHot carcass weight), as proposed by Guidoni et al. (2007).

The pH assessment was performed 45 min and 24 h after slaughter in the longissimus thoracis at the last rib and in the semimembranosus by using a portable digital pH meter. Twenty-four hours after slaughter, marbling and color scores (NPPC, 1999) as well as color measured by the CIELAB system (coordinates L*, a* and b*, Minolta Camera Ltda., Japan) were evaluated in both muscles. Muscle samples between, 8 and 12 g, were collected for drip loss evaluation. Samples were weighed, placed in meat juice containers, refrigerated at 2-4 °C for 48 h and re-weighed. Drip loss was calculated from the difference between the initial and final sample weight, and expressed as a percentage loss (Honikel, 1998).

At the end of the experiment all pigs were weighed and, after 12 h of fasting, were transported to a commercial slaughterhouse, located approximately 15 km from the experimental facilities. Pigs were slaughtered after 4 h of lairage, following the standard procedure of the slaughterhouse. After exsanguination and evisceration the car- casses were driven to a chilling room set for 2-4 °C. Twenty four hours after slaughter, a cut was made between the last thoracic and the first lumbar vertebra of the left half carcass, where the loin eye area and the fat area were evaluated by drawing the outline of the muscle and the fat at the tenth rib on a paper, according to the Brazilian Method of Pig Carcass Grading System (ABCS, 1973). The drawings were scanned and the loin eye area and fat area, as well as the muscle depth and backfat

thickness, were determined using the Rhinoceros® 4.0 software (Robert McNeel and Associates, 2007). From these data the lean percentage (LP) of pig carcasses was calculated, according to the following equation LP=58.408-(0.5886xBackfat)+(0.1739xMuscle depth) (0.0189xHot carcass weight), as proposed by Guidoni et al. (2007).

The pH assessment was performed 45 min and 24 h after slaughter in the longissimus thoracis at the last rib and in the semimembranosus by using a portable digital pH meter. Twenty-four hours after slaughter, marbling and color scores (NPPC, 1999) as well as color measured by the CIELAB system (coordinates L*, a* and b*, Minolta Camera Ltda., Japan) were evaluated in both muscles. Muscle samples between, 8 and 12 g, were collected for drip loss evaluation. Samples were weighed, placed in meat juice containers, refrigerated at 2-4 °C for 48 h and re-weighed. Drip loss was calculated from the difference between the initial and final sample weight, and expressed as a percentage loss (Honikel, 1998).
20: The cost of diets was determined based on the ingredient prices during the trial period (internal report of Embrapa Suínos e Aves). The average feed cost (R$/kg of weight gain) during each phase was calculated by multiplying the FCR by the cost of the diet (R$/kg).

The estimated N and P balance was based on a mass balance approach as proposed by Rigolot et al. (2010). Nitrogen and P intake were obtained from feed intake and feed analysis. Nitrogen in body tissues was determined from live weight and lean meat content (Rigolot et al., 2010). Phosphorus in body tissues was calculated according to Rigolot et al. (2010).
21: A farm-to-gate life cycle assessment (LCA) was performed. The LCA considered the process of pig fattening, including crop production, grain drying and processing, production and transport of feed ingre- dients, feed production at the feed factory, transport of the feed to the farm, growing to finishing pig production, and manure storage, transport and spreading, as developed in previous studies (Nguyen et al., 2010, 2011; Garcia-Launay et al., 2014).

The pig production system considered was a typical conventional growing-finishing pig farm located in Santa Catarina State (as de- scribed by Cherubini et al. (2015)). For soybean and maize, we hypothesized that the grains came from the main producing areas in the South. For transport, we used the methodology described by Prudêncio da Silva et al. (2010). The volume of slurry produced was measured through collector rails in each experimental unit. Temperature data recorded during the trial were used to calculate slurry methane emission according to IPCC (2006). The models and system boundaries were derived from Garcia-Launay et al. (2014).

LCA was performed according to the CML 2001 (baseline) method V3.02 as implemented in Simapro® software, version 8.05 (PRé Consultant, 2014). We added the following categories: land occupation from CML 2001 (all categories) version 2.04 and total cumulative energy demand version 1.8 (non-renewable fossil+nuclear). Thus, we considered the potentials impacts of pig production on climate change (CC, kg CO2-eq.), eutrophication potential (EU, g PO4-eq.), acidifica- tion potential (AC, g SO2-eq.), terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE, g 1.4-DCB- eq.), cumulative energy demand (CED, MJ), and land occupation (LO, n2.year). The CC was calculated according to the 100-year global warming potential factors expressed in kg CO2-eq.

LCA calculations were performed for each experimental unit according to its individual performance and excretion. These calcula- tions were performed using a model developed with SAS software (SAS Inst. Inc., Cary, NC). The functional unit considered was one kg of body weight gain (BWG) over the fattening period.

23: Feeds and feed ingredients were analyzed following the methodol- ogy proposed in AOAC (1990) for dry matter (method 950.46) and CP (nitrogenx6.25) according to Kjeldahl (method 984.13). The AA were analyzed by high-performance liquid chromatography technique ac- cording to the method of Jones and Gilligan (1983). Phosphorus was analyzed by using UV-Vis spectrophotometry (model Cary 50°, Varian Inc). Limestone and dicalcium phosphate were analyzed for phos- phorus (UV-Vis spectrophotometry), calcium and magnesium (by atomic absorption spectroscopy; model SpectrAA 220°, Varian Inc).
24: Growth performance, carcass traits and meat quality data, as well as environmental impacts were subjected to variance analysis using the GLM procedure of SAS. The statistical model included the effects of treatment, sex and block, as well as the interactions between treatment and sex. Differences were considered significant if P < 0.05. All analyses were performed using SAS version 9.2 (SAS Inst. Inc., Cary, NC).
25: Pigs consumed feed and gained weight according to the expected performance of the genotype throughout the entire trial. The environ- mental temperature stayed within the thermal comfort zone for pigs, with average values ranging between 24 and 20 °C in growing and finishing phases, respectively, with an average relative humidity between 76% and 79%, respectively.
26: The difference in crude protein and SID AA requirements between ST and LN diets are presented in Table 1. Compared to ST, the LN strategy allowed the dietary CP content to be reduced by 19%, 15%, 11% and 7.4% in Phases 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively. Although the magnitude of CP reduction was decreasing between phases, for SID lysine, the first limiting AA, it was increasing, with 8.1%, 18%, 19% and 22% reduction in Phases 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively. These differences in dietary CP were mainly associated with higher soybean meal content in the ST diet compared to the LN diets, and conversely a lower maize content. The soybean oil content of the ST diet was also higher, in order to achieve the same ME content in the ST and LN diets.

The addition of phytase enzyme reduced the total phosphorus content of LN diets compared to ST diets by 8.9% and 15% in Phase 3 and 4, respectively. This represented a reduction of 28% and 29% of phosphate inclusion, respectively. This reduction was expected, since the improvement in phosphorus digestibility through phytase addition is a consolidated approach feeding strategy (Lei and Stahl, 2001; Adeola et al., 2011).
27: The difference in crude protein and SID AA requirements between ST and LN diets are presented in Table 1. Compared to ST, the LN strategy allowed the dietary CP content to be reduced by 19%, 15%, 11% and 7.4% in Phases 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively. Although the magnitude of CP reduction was decreasing between phases, for SID lysine, the first limiting AA, it was increasing, with 8.1%, 18%, 19% and 22% reduction in Phases 1, 2, 3 and 4, respectively. These differences in dietary CP were mainly associated with higher soybean meal content in the ST diet compared to the LN diets, and conversely a lower maize content. The soybean oil content of the ST diet was also higher, in order to achieve the same ME content in the ST and LN diets.

The addition of phytase enzyme reduced the total phosphorus content of LN diets compared to ST diets by 8.9% and 15% in Phase 3 and 4, respectively. This represented a reduction of 28% and 29% of phosphate inclusion, respectively. This reduction was expected, since the improvement in phosphorus digestibility through phytase addition is a consolidated approach feeding strategy (Lei and Stahl, 2001; Adeola et al., 2011).
28: There was no effect 0.10) of treatments on growth performance and body traits (Table 3) except for ADFI which tended to be lower by about 5.0% in LN pigs during the finishing period, whereas no significant difference was observed during growing or over the whole period. This indicates that the published average recommendations for CP and SID AA content from the Brazilian tables overestimate the real requirements of the pigs in our experimental conditions, although the achieved growth rate was high kg/d ADG) and similar to the hypotheses used in Rostagno et al. (2011). These results are in agreement with many literature results reviewed by Dourmad and Jondreville (2007) indicating that it is possible to reduce the dietary CP without affecting growth performance and carcass composition as soon as daily SID AA supplies are adequate. However, this requires precise

determination of the requirements over time and according to animal performance, which is possible with the use of decision support tools (van Milgen et al., 2008; NRC, 2012). The sex affected (P < 0.05) all the performance variables throughout the trial. Barrows showed higher ADFI and ADG than gilts. This effect was expected, since barrows consume more feed and generally achieve greater weight gain than gilts (Morales et al., 2011). Conversely, the FCR during the finishing phase was better in gilts than in barrows in line with a higher protein and lower fat deposition in gilts.

Fig. 1 shows a comparison between the nutrient intake in this study, with that recommended in the Brazilian Tables (Rostagno et al., 2011) and NRC (2012). We used the nutrient recommendation (in g per kg) in these tables multiplied by the expected feed intake for each phase. For ST, although diets were formulated with Brazilian recom- mendations, pigs consumed slightly less energy and nutrient than expected (93-95% of recommendations for ME, digestible P, CP and SID lysine, methionine and threonine) except for SID tryptophan and valine, for which the intake was equal or slightly above recommenda- tions 101-109%. For all nutrients, the daily intake of ST pigs was higher than recommended by NRC (2012) (115-141% of NRC recommendation for daily ME, digestible P, CP and SID AA).

Regarding LN diets, except for digestible P, the nutrient intake was considerably lower than that proposed in Brazilian tables (Fig. 1). For CP and SID lysine, methionine, threonine, tryptophan and valine, the daily intake amounted to 78%, 76%, 76%, 78%, 82% and 90%, respectively, of the values proposed in Brazilian tables. Conversely, the SID AA intake of LN pigs was close to the value recommended by NRC, i.e., 93%, 99%, 101% and 103% for SID lysine, methionine, threonine and tryptophan, respectively. For CP, the daily intake of LN pigs was lower than for ST, but still higher than the NRC minimum requirement. In general, supplies calculated from the InraPorc model were rather close to NRC and lower than Brazilian table recommenda- tions. Other authors also observed that the nutritional requirements of Brazilian pigs are lower than those proposed by Rostagno et al. (2011), such as for SID tryptophan and lysine for barrows from 15 to 30 kg (Pasquetti et al., 2015) or for methionine+cysteine for barrows and gilts from 75 to 100 kg (Sangali, 2016). This may be related to the use of safety margins when defining average recommendations at national level.
29: There was no effect 0.10) of treatments on growth performance and body traits (Table 3) except for ADFI which tended to be lower by about 5.0% in LN pigs during the finishing period, whereas no significant difference was observed during growing or over the whole period. This indicates that the published average recommendations for CP and SID AA content from the Brazilian tables overestimate the real requirements of the pigs in our experimental conditions, although the achieved growth rate was high kg/d ADG) and similar to the hypotheses used in Rostagno et al. (2011). These results are in agreement with many literature results reviewed by Dourmad and Jondreville (2007) indicating that it is possible to reduce the dietary CP without affecting growth performance and carcass composition as soon as daily SID AA supplies are adequate. However, this requires precise

determination of the requirements over time and according to animal performance, which is possible with the use of decision support tools (van Milgen et al., 2008; NRC, 2012). The sex affected (P < 0.05) all the performance variables throughout the trial. Barrows showed higher ADFI and ADG than gilts. This effect was expected, since barrows consume more feed and generally achieve greater weight gain than gilts (Morales et al., 2011). Conversely, the FCR during the finishing phase was better in gilts than in barrows in line with a higher protein and lower fat deposition in gilts.

32: Carcass traits were not affected 0.05) by dietary treatments; neither were the meat quality traits evaluated at longissimus thoracis (Table 4). There was an effect on color < 0.05) at the semimem- branosus, LN pigs showing redder meat (greater a* value) than ST pigs. In spite of this difference, the color values were within the range of meat quality standards (NPPC, 1999).

Backfat thickness was affected by sex 0.05), with higher values for barrows than gilts. This result is in agreement with those reported in the literature, in which gilts have generally a lower average backfat thickness than barrows (Borosky et al., 2011). These differences are related to the absence of sexual hormones in barrows. Since testoster- one is an anabolic hormone, it is responsible for decreasing protein breakdown and promoting muscle protein deposition (Guyton and Hall, 2008). This may explain the result for lean percentage, which was logically higher (P < 0.05) in gilts, due to their greater protein and lower fat deposition capacity.

Barrows showed redder meat than gilts, with a greater a* value (P < 0.05). In addition, there was a sex-treatment interaction (P 0.05) on pH value 24 h after slaughter, ST barrows showing slightly higher pH than gilts whereas there was no difference in LN pigs. However, the average value for all treatments remains within pork quality optimal standards reported by the National Pork Producers Council (NPPC, 1999).

38: The average feed cost per kg BWG was 6.8% lower (P < 0.001) for LN than for ST treatment (Table 5). This result was expected since LN diets contained less soybean meal, which is more expensive than maize. Pomar et al. (2014) and Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) also reported a reduction in feed cost when pigs were fed closer to their requirement with a low protein diet.

Other costs are involved in pig production (labor, veterinary expenses, transportation, taxes, depreciation, etc.), but since feed represents 70% of total production cost, any reduction in feed cost may provide a great economic advantage. Furthermore, protein sources contribute to about 25% of total feed cost. So, since the LN strategy allowed for a reduction in the protein content, a lower feed cost can be expected with such a strategy in most commercial situations.

Calculated N and P balance are presented in Table 5. Nitrogen and P intake were 16% and 9.4% higher for ST compared to LN pigs. For N, this is related to the reduction in CP content of LN diets resulting from the better adjustment of supplies. For P, the lower intake in the LN treatment is related to the reduction in total dietary P content and phytase supplementation in the finishing diets. Although phytase has

not been added to the growing diets, its supplementation in the finishing phase was enough to reduce the P intake over the entire period, since this is the phase when pigs consume the greatest amount of feed. This result is in agreement with the results reported in previous studies (Cromwell et al., 1993; Harper et al., 1997; Ludke et al., 2002).

There was no difference (P 0.05) between treatments for nutrient retention and consequently N and P excretion was lower (P < 0.001) by about 25% and 15%, respectively, for pigs fed with LN diets. SID AA fed in excess to the requirement for maximal protein retention are oxidized to produce energy, leading to an increased excretion of urea (McLeod, 1997). In the same way, P fed in excess or not digested is excreted in urine or feces (Létourneau-Montminy et al., 2015). Pomar et al. (2014) reported that daily multiphase feeding (with low nutrient concentra- tion) reduced the N and P intake by 7.3% and 4.4%, respectively, and by 13% and 6.6% the N and P excreted, respectively.

Le Bellego et al. (2002) calculated that for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction, N excretion can be expected to decrease by 10%. Andretta et al. (2014) reported slightly lower values with 6.6-8.4% reduction in N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction. Our study corroborates these results with a reduction of 11% of N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction.

not been added to the growing diets, its supplementation in the finishing phase was enough to reduce the P intake over the entire period, since this is the phase when pigs consume the greatest amount of feed. This result is in agreement with the results reported in previous studies (Cromwell et al., 1993; Harper et al., 1997; Ludke et al., 2002).

There was no difference (P 0.05) between treatments for nutrient retention and consequently N and P excretion was lower (P < 0.001) by about 25% and 15%, respectively, for pigs fed with LN diets. SID AA fed in excess to the requirement for maximal protein retention are oxidized to produce energy, leading to an increased excretion of urea (McLeod, 1997). In the same way, P fed in excess or not digested is excreted in urine or feces (Létourneau-Montminy et al., 2015). Pomar et al. (2014) reported that daily multiphase feeding (with low nutrient concentra- tion) reduced the N and P intake by 7.3% and 4.4%, respectively, and by 13% and 6.6% the N and P excreted, respectively.

Le Bellego et al. (2002) calculated that for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction, N excretion can be expected to decrease by 10%. Andretta et al. (2014) reported slightly lower values with 6.6-8.4% reduction in N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction. Our study corroborates these results with a reduction of 11% of N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction.

Sex affected (P < 0.05) both feed costs and N and P excretion (Table 5), gilts having lower values than barrows. This was expected since all these variables are related to feed intake and barrows consume more than gilts (see Table 3). Furthermore, gilts showed higher protein and less fat deposition than barrows suggesting that barrows were overfed.
40: The average feed cost per kg BWG was 6.8% lower (P < 0.001) for LN than for ST treatment (Table 5). This result was expected since LN diets contained less soybean meal, which is more expensive than maize. Pomar et al. (2014) and Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) also reported a reduction in feed cost when pigs were fed closer to their requirement with a low protein diet.

Other costs are involved in pig production (labor, veterinary expenses, transportation, taxes, depreciation, etc.), but since feed represents 70% of total production cost, any reduction in feed cost may provide a great economic advantage. Furthermore, protein sources contribute to about 25% of total feed cost. So, since the LN strategy allowed for a reduction in the protein content, a lower feed cost can be expected with such a strategy in most commercial situations.

In the same way, P fed in excess or not digested is excreted in urine or feces (Létourneau-Montminy et al., 2015). Pomar et al. (2014) reported that daily multiphase feeding (with low nutrient concentra- tion) reduced the N and P intake by 7.3% and 4.4%, respectively, and by 13% and 6.6% the N and P excreted, respectively.

Le Bellego et al. (2002) calculated that for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction, N excretion can be expected to decrease by 10%. Andretta et al. (2014) reported slightly lower values with 6.6-8.4% reduction in N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction. Our study corroborates these results with a reduction of 11% of N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction.

Sex affected (P < 0.05) both feed costs and N and P excretion (Table 5), gilts having lower values than barrows. This was expected since all these variables are related to feed intake and barrows consume more than gilts (see Table 3). Furthermore, gilts showed higher protein and less fat deposition than barrows suggesting that barrows were overfed.
44: The average feed cost per kg BWG was 6.8% lower (P < 0.001) for LN than for ST treatment (Table 5). This result was expected since LN diets contained less soybean meal, which is more expensive than maize. Pomar et al. (2014) and Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) also reported a reduction in feed cost when pigs were fed closer to their requirement with a low protein diet.

Other costs are involved in pig production (labor, veterinary expenses, transportation, taxes, depreciation, etc.), but since feed represents 70% of total production cost, any reduction in feed cost may provide a great economic advantage. Furthermore, protein sources contribute to about 25% of total feed cost. So, since the LN strategy allowed for a reduction in the protein content, a lower feed cost can be expected with such a strategy in most commercial situations.

Calculated N and P balance are presented in Table 5. Nitrogen and P intake were 16% and 9.4% higher for ST compared to LN pigs. For N, this is related to the reduction in CP content of LN diets resulting from the better adjustment of supplies. For P, the lower intake in the LN treatment is related to the reduction in total dietary P content and phytase supplementation in the finishing diets. Although phytase has

not been added to the growing diets, its supplementation in the finishing phase was enough to reduce the P intake over the entire period, since this is the phase when pigs consume the greatest amount of feed. This result is in agreement with the results reported in previous studies (Cromwell et al., 1993; Harper et al., 1997; Ludke et al., 2002).

There was no difference (P 0.05) between treatments for nutrient retention and consequently N and P excretion was lower (P < 0.001) by about 25% and 15%, respectively, for pigs fed with LN diets. SID AA fed in excess to the requirement for maximal protein retention are oxidized to produce energy, leading to an increased excretion of urea (McLeod, 1997). In the same way, P fed in excess or not digested is excreted in urine or feces (Létourneau-Montminy et al., 2015). Pomar et al. (2014) reported that daily multiphase feeding (with low nutrient concentra- tion) reduced the N and P intake by 7.3% and 4.4%, respectively, and by 13% and 6.6% the N and P excreted, respectively.

Le Bellego et al. (2002) calculated that for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction, N excretion can be expected to decrease by 10%. Andretta et al. (2014) reported slightly lower values with 6.6-8.4% reduction in N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction. Our study corroborates these results with a reduction of 11% of N excretion for each percentage unit of dietary CP reduction.

Sex affected (P < 0.05) both feed costs and N and P excretion (Table 5), gilts having lower values than barrows. This was expected since all these variables are related to feed intake and barrows consume more than gilts (see Table 3). Furthermore, gilts showed higher protein and less fat deposition than barrows suggesting that barrows were overfed.
46: The CC impact was reduced by 4% in the LN compared to the ST treatment (P=0.02) and was lower in gilts than in barrows (P=0.01; Table 6). Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) assessed the environmental impact of pig production in France. In the same way as in the present study, these authors found that reducing the dietary CP by better adapting supplies to requirements, or by increasing the incorporation of feed-use AA, reduced the CC impact by about 3% for each percentage unit of CP content. The effect of reducing CP on carbon emissions is mainly the result of reducing the incorporation of soybean meal, which has a higher impact than the cereal fraction of the diet. As the soybean

was assumed to be produced in South Brazil without deforestation, and consequently with low CC impact (Prudêncio da Silva et al., 2010), it could be expected that the difference between treatments would have been much more pronounced with soybean from areas with recent deforestation as shown by Monteiro et al. (2016) both for Brazil and for France.

Acidification and EU potential were reduced by about 8% and 10%, respectively, when pigs were fed with the LN diet compared to the ST diet 0.05), these being lower impacts in gilts than in barrows (P < 0.01). The lower impact when pigs were fed with LN diets was due to the lower N and P excretion (see Table 5). Since both N and P contribute to EU, and N contributes to AC by ammonia emissions (Guinée et al., 2002), the reduction in excretion minimized the NH3 emissions from animal housing, manure management and field appli- cation. Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) also found that the reduction in dietary CP content caused by increasing the incorporation of crystalline AA, reduced the AC and EU impacts by about 31% and 20%, respectively. In our trial, the magnitude of the effect was higher for the EU category, which could be related to the phytase addition in
Table 5 Feed costs and simulated nitrogen and phosphorus balance of growing-finishing pigs fed from 30 to 130 kg BW with standard nutrient diets (ST) or with low nutrient diets (LN).
Table 6 Environmental impacts per kg of body weight gain, at the farm gate, of growing-finishing pigs fed from 30 to 130 kg BW with standard nutrient diets (ST) or with low nutrient diets (LN).
CC, Climate change. AC, Acidification. EU, Eutrophication. CED, Cumulative energy demand. TE, Terrestrial ecotoxicity. LO, Land occupation.

finisher diets. Nielsen and Wenzel (2007) found that incorporating phytase instead of inorganic phosphate supplementation could poten- tially reduce the AC by 110% and EU by 700% in a pig production unit in Denmark.

Cumulative energy demand impact was not affected by the treat- ments (P=0.88). Since this category quantifies the energy consumption throughout all the steps of pig production, and the main feedstuffs used in the diets, i.e., maize and soybean meal, come from the same place, it was expected that CED would not change dramatically between treatments. Ogino et al. (2013) assessed the environmental impact of pig production in Japan. They found that reducing the dietary CP content by the addition of crystalline AA was not efficient in lowering energy consumption. These authors suggested that the low protein diet technique mainly reduced N20 emissions, but is not relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufacturing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. In the same way as in the present study, these authors found that the difference in CED between the low protein diet and the conventional diet was smaller than 3%. In our trial, the difference between the LN and ST diets was smaller than 1%.

The terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE) impact was reduced by 4% in the LN treatment (P=0.01) and was higher in barrows than in gilts (P < 0.01). This result was associated with the low TE impact of maize, and the LN diets allowed more maize inclusion than ST diets. Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) found that formulations with feed-use AA, without CP restric- tion, reduced the TE impact by about 13% compared to formulations without AA addition, but in their study wheat and not maize was the main cereal incorporated in the diet.

The impact under land occupation (LO) was reduced by about 9% in the LN compared to the ST treatment (P < 0.01), and gilts showed lower LO impact than barrows (P < 0.01). The decrease of LO was the result of the lower soybean inclusion and higher maize inclusion in LN diets. Prudêncio da Silva et al. (2014) reported a LO of soybean meal more than twice as high as for maize in Brazilian grain production. In the same way as in the present study, Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) found that reducing soybean meal inclusion in pig diets through crystalline AA, decreased the LO by 3%. This suggests that incorporat- ing AA leads to a moderate reduction in land occupation.

Since both N and P contribute to EU, and N contributes to AC by ammonia emissions (Guinée et al., 2002), the reduction in excretion minimized the NH3 emissions from animal housing, manure management and field appli- cation. Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) also found that the reduction in dietary CP content caused by increasing the incorporation of crystalline AA, reduced the AC and EU impacts by about 31% and 20%, respectively. In our trial, the magnitude of the effect was higher for the EU category, which could be related to the phytase addition in
Table 5 Feed costs and simulated nitrogen and phosphorus balance of growing-finishing pigs fed from 30 to 130 kg BW with standard nutrient diets (ST) or with low nutrient diets (LN).
Table 6 Environmental impacts per kg of body weight gain, at the farm gate, of growing-finishing pigs fed from 30 to 130 kg BW with standard nutrient diets (ST) or with low nutrient diets (LN).
CC, Climate change. AC, Acidification. EU, Eutrophication. CED, Cumulative energy demand. TE, Terrestrial ecotoxicity. LO, Land occupation.

finisher diets. Nielsen and Wenzel (2007) found that incorporating phytase instead of inorganic phosphate supplementation could poten- tially reduce the AC by 110% and EU by 700% in a pig production unit in Denmark.

Cumulative energy demand impact was not affected by the treat- ments (P=0.88). Since this category quantifies the energy consumption throughout all the steps of pig production, and the main feedstuffs used in the diets, i.e., maize and soybean meal, come from the same place, it was expected that CED would not change dramatically between treatments. Ogino et al. (2013) assessed the environmental impact of pig production in Japan. They found that reducing the dietary CP content by the addition of crystalline AA was not efficient in lowering energy consumption. These authors suggested that the low protein diet technique mainly reduced N20 emissions, but is not relevant to energy consumption, while AA manufacturing consumed more energy than crop production per unit amount. In the same way as in the present study, these authors found that the difference in CED between the low protein diet and the conventional diet was smaller than 3%. In our trial, the difference between the LN and ST diets was smaller than 1%.

The terrestrial ecotoxicity (TE) impact was reduced by 4% in the LN treatment (P=0.01) and was higher in barrows than in gilts (P < 0.01). This result was associated with the low TE impact of maize, and the LN diets allowed more maize inclusion than ST diets. Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) found that formulations with feed-use AA, without CP restric- tion, reduced the TE impact by about 13% compared to formulations without AA addition, but in their study wheat and not maize was the main cereal incorporated in the diet.

The impact under land occupation (LO) was reduced by about 9% in the LN compared to the ST treatment (P < 0.01), and gilts showed lower LO impact than barrows (P < 0.01). The decrease of LO was the result of the lower soybean inclusion and higher maize inclusion in LN diets. Prudêncio da Silva et al. (2014) reported a LO of soybean meal more than twice as high as for maize in Brazilian grain production. In the same way as in the present study, Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) found that reducing soybean meal inclusion in pig diets through crystalline AA, decreased the LO by 3%. This suggests that incorporat- ing AA leads to a moderate reduction in land occupation.
49: The CC impact was reduced by 4% in the LN compared to the ST treatment (P=0.02) and was lower in gilts than in barrows (P=0.01; Table 6). Garcia-Launay et al. (2014) assessed the environmental impact of pig production in France. In the same way as in the present study, these authors found that reducing the dietary CP by better adapting supplies to requirements, or by increasing the incorporation of feed-use AA, reduced the CC impact by about 3% for each percentage unit of CP content. The effect of reducing CP on carbon emissions is mainly the result of reducing the incorporation of soybean meal, which has a higher impact than the cereal fraction of the diet. As the soybean

was assumed to be produced in South Brazil without deforestation, and consequently with low CC impact (Prudêncio da Silva et al., 2010), it could be expected that the difference between treatments would have been much more pronounced with soybean from areas with recent deforestation as shown by Monteiro et al. (2016) both for Brazil and for France.

54: The results of this trial demonstrate that the use of adjusted dietary levels of nutrients instead of standard average recommendations, as commonly used in Brazil, appears an efficient strategy to improve economic and environmental sustainability of pig production. This is now possible with the use decision support tools such as InraPorc® and NRC (2012). However, to be implemented with success, this requires a precise characterization of animals profiles in terms of feed intake and growth potential, based on measurements in practical conditions.
